message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/
**Title:** Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013)
**Found via:** formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind humans. Specifically: (1) What syllable rates / speech rates are discussed for ultra-fast speech and what rates blind people can comprehend vs sighted people? (2) What does the study say about training effects on comprehending time-compressed/ultra-fast speech? (3) Any quantitative comprehension figures, word-rate/syllable-rate numbers, or training improvement percentages. (4) Any mention of formant synthesis, TTS, screen readers, or how the speech stimuli were generated/time-compressed. (5) The publication date and authors/journal. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding.

content.content:
# Key Findings on Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Blind Humans

## (1) Syllable Rates: Blind vs. Sighted Comprehension

**Ultra-fast speech rates discussed:**
- "understanding spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per seconds (s)—exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"

**Study rates tested:**
- Moderately fast: 8 syl/s
- Ultra-fast target: 18 syl/s (with intermediate testing at 13 syl/s)

---

## (2) Training Effects on Time-Compressed Speech Comprehension

**Overall training success:**
- "Four of six participants showed—independently from residual visual functions—considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)"

**Pre vs. post-training performance:**
- Prior to training: "the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances"
- After ~6 months training: subjects achieving "an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points"

---

## (3) Quantitative Comprehension Figures

**Baseline performance:**
- At 8 syl/s pre-training: "mean = 97%, SE = 6.94%" (post-training, all subjects)
- At 18 syl/s pre-training: below 14% for all participants
- At 18 syl/s post-training: approximately 70 percentage points improvement for successful learners

---

## (4) Speech Stimulus Generation Methods

**Text-to-speech system used:**
- "screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence')"
- "formant synthesizer 'eloquence' implemented in the synthesizer JAWS"
- Stimuli creation: "All these test items had a duration of ca. 4 s after text-to-speech conversion"

---

## (5) Publication Information

**Citation details:**
- **Journal:** Frontiers in Human Neuroscience
- **Date:** Published October 23, 2013
- **Authors:** Susanne Dietrich, Ingo Hertrich, Hermann Ackermann
- **DOI:** 10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701

content.text: The fetch succeeded with rich, relevant content. This is a peer-reviewed primary research article directly relevant to multiple angles of the research question (blind power users, formant synthesis/Eloquence, ultra-fast speech rates, training effects).

claims.claim: Trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at up to about 22 syllables/second, far exceeding the ~8 syllables/second maximum of untrained listeners.
claims.quote: understanding spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per seconds (s)—exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)

claims.claim: The ultra-fast speech stimuli used by these blind power listeners were generated with the formant synthesizer 'Eloquence' running in the JAWS screen reader, directly evidencing the blind community's reliance on formant synthesis at high speed.
claims.quote: formant synthesizer 'eloquence' implemented in the synthesizer JAWS

claims.claim: Before training, comprehension of 18 syl/s ultra-fast speech was near zero (correctly reproduced syllables below 14%), but after ~6 months of training four of six participants reached about 70 percentage points correct, demonstrating a large measurable trainability effect.
claims.quote: Four of six participants showed—independently from residual visual functions—considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)

claims.claim: Prior to training, all participants performed below 14% correct on ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech, quantifying the baseline difficulty of comprehension at extreme rates without adaptation.
claims.quote: the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances

claims.claim: At a moderately fast rate of 8 syl/s, comprehension remained near ceiling (mean ~97%), indicating that intelligibility breakdown is rate-dependent and emerges only at extreme syllable rates well above normal speech.

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from this peer-reviewed primary research article (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013, Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann). The source is highly relevant: it directly documents that blind power listeners use the formant synthesizer Eloquence (via JAWS) at extreme speeds, quantifies the 22 syl/s ceiling vs ~8 syl/s untrained baseline, and shows large trainability gains (from <14% to ~70% correct after ~6 months). This bears most directly on research angles (2) formant synthesis vs neural at extreme speed and (1) acoustic properties / rate ceilings.
